data.title: Biggest stock movers Tuesday: BBBY, CLS, and more
data.description:
Stock futures were cautious Tuesday morning as investors weighed reports that President Donald Trump was not satisfied with Iran’s latest proposal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and defer nuclear talks.

Here are some of Tuesday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Bed Bath & Beyond (BBBY) +16% - Shares soared after the company reported its first meaningful revenue growth in nearly five years (+9.4% Y/Y, adjusted), alongside its sixth straight quarter of adjusted EBITDA improvement. The upside was driven by stronger customer engagement, higher conversion, and improved average order value, while investors also reacted positively to its acquisition strategy, including Kirkland’s and planned deals for The Container Store and F9 Brands aimed at building an integrated retail ecosystem. Management’s focus on cost synergies (>$60M) and operational consolidation further supported the bullish sentiment.
  • LendingClub Corporation (LC) +13% - Shares jumped after Q1 results beat expectations across the board, driven by 31% Y/Y growth in loan originations and a lower provision for credit losses following an accounting change. The company also issued solid Q2 guidance and reaffirmed its full-year outlook, reinforcing confidence in sustained growth and improving profitability.

Biggest stock losers

  • Celestica (CLS) -7% - Shares dipped despite posting a strong Q1 beat and raising guidance, as investor expectations appeared even higher following the stock’s prior run. The company delivered robust 52.8% Y/Y revenue growth and topped EPS estimates while also lifting its 2026 outlook and issuing upbeat Q2 guidance. However, the selloff suggests profit-taking and concerns that much of the strong growth and AI-driven momentum were already priced in.
  • NOV (NOV) -5% - Shares fell after Q1 results showed a GAAP EPS miss at $0.05, while revenue of $2.05B (-2.4% Y/Y) came in line with estimates. The company returned $100M to shareholders through buybacks and dividends but guided Q2 revenue to decline 4%–6% Y/Y with adjusted EBITDA of $185M–$215M, citing ongoing uncertainty and disruptions tied to the Middle East conflict that impacted operations, logistics, and costs during the quarter.

data.title: Poet's stock continues to tumble after contract cancellation by Marvell
data.description:
Shares of Poet Technologies continued to tumble premarket on Tuesday after the stock plummeted about 47% on Monday following disclosure about the cancellation of purchase orders.

On Monday, Canadian company Poet — which provides integrated photonic solutions for AI and optical networking markets — announced the cancellation of all purchase orders received by it from Celestial AI, which has been acquired by Marvell Technology (MRVL). The cancellation orders also included initial production units first disclosed in April 2023.

Poet's stock fell about 4% premarket on Tuesday, while Marvell's shares declined nearly 3%.

Marvell Technology had completed its $3.25B cash-and-stock acquisition of Celestial AI in February.

data.title: Foreign automakers may pull their cheapest cars from U.S. without USMCA - report
data.description:
Foreign automakers warned the Trump administration that they may pull their cheapest car models out of the U.S. if its free trade deal with Mexico and Canada isn't renewed or is watered down, The Wall Street Journal reported, citing people familiar with the talks.

Toyota (TM), Honda (HMC) and Nissan (NSANY) are among the few companies that offer new models of small, affordable cars in the U.S. as Detroit automakers focus more on SUVs and trucks.

Cars like the Toyota Corolla and Honda Civic are manufactured in the U.S. but require parts from all three North American countries.

Some foreign automakers may not be able to make and sell such cars for the U.S. market if the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement is scrapped or if its renewed version doesn't significantly lower auto tariffs.

Honda (HMC) said it would continue selling its compact Civic in the U.S. even without a trade deal, although the economics of doing so would become more difficult without the USMCA.

Eight of the 10 cheapest new car models in the U.S. are made by foreign automakers, according to online car-shopping advisor Edmunds, and the other two are compact SUVs made by General Motors (GM) in South Korea.

The Trump administration signaled that any renewed USMCA must have tougher rules to limit Chinese parts in qualifying cars and reshore U.S. manufacturing. It hasn't committed to providing tariff-free treatment for vehicles in a revised USMCA. 

Foreign automakers are already losing money because of President Trump's tariffs on auto parts, finished automobiles, steel and aluminum. The tariffs, combined with higher labor and other costs in the U.S., make it unprofitable to build their cheapest models in the U.S.

Executives at Nissan (NSANY), Toyota (TM) and Honda (HMC) have backed extending the USMCA to help ease costs. The deal is due for joint review in July.

data.title: Nissan gains after lifting outlook on cost cuts, FX
data.description:
Nissan Motor (NSANY) revised its earnings outlook for fiscal 2025, which ends on March 31, 2026, flipping from a projected operating loss of ¥60B to a profit of ¥50B. The stock rose as much as about 6.5% in Tokyo trading, marking its biggest intraday gain in over two months.

The carmaker attributed this turnaround to the removal of U.S. emissions-related charges, favorable foreign exchange effects, and stronger cost controls from its ongoing restructuring.

Its revenue is now projected to reach ¥12T for the year ending March 31, 2026, up from a prior estimate of ¥11.9T, supported in part by currency tailwinds. 

However, Nissan (NSANY) still expects a net loss of ¥550B, though this represents a narrowing from its earlier forecast of a ¥650B deficit.

data.title: EU regulator proposes measures to Google for rival AI services on Android
data.description:
The European Commission said it sent draft measures to Google, which the Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) unit should implement to ensure that third parties, such as online search rivals and AI developers, have effective access and interoperability with key capabilities of Android.

The Commission noted that the proposed measures aim to ensure that competing AI services can effectively interact with applications on users' Android devices and execute tasks accordingly, such as sending an email using the user's preferred email app.

"Today’s proposed measures will give more choice to Android users about the AI services they use and integrate in their phone, including from the vast range of AI services that compete with Google’s own AI," said Teresa Ribera, European Commissioner for Competition and executive vice-president for a Clean, Just and Competitive Transition. 

Currently, Google largely reserves these capabilities for use by its own AI offerings on Android phones and tablets. For example, the measures would allow competing AI services to be easily activated by users, using a custom 'wake word,' a phrase that the user can speak to activate an AI service, the antitrust regulator added.

The proposed measures will also enable competing providers of AI services to innovate and offer integrated AI experiences to users on Android phones and tablets, along with Google' own AI services, such as Gemini, according to the Commission.

Google did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Google criticized the EU proposal, saying Android has an open ecosystem enabling AI assistants to thrive and device makers to have full autonomy to customize their AI services, Reuters reported.

"This ​unwarranted intervention would strip away that autonomy, mandate access to sensitive hardware and device ​permissions; unnecessarily driving up costs while undermining critical privacy and security protections for European users," said Clare ‌Kelly, ⁠the company's Senior Competition Counsel, to Reuters.

These preliminary findings are part of the proceedings the regulator started in January under the Digital Markets Act, or DMA.

Interested parties have until May 13 to submit their views on the draft measures. 

Earlier this month, the European Commission sent preliminary findings to Google outlining proposed measures under the DMA which the company should allow third-party search engines to access Google search data, including that of AI chatbots with search functionalities.

The DMA is a legislation that establishes a set of objective criteria to qualify a large online platform as a "gatekeeper" and ensure that they behave fairly online and leave room for contestability.

data.title: Rivian CEO's $402M pay package hinges on aggressive growth targets
data.description:
Rivian Automotive (RIVN) CEO RJ Scaringe received a compensation package of about $402.6M for 2025, driven largely by a newly approved equity award designed to tie his long-term pay to the company’s performance, according to a regulatory filing on Monday. 

The package includes roughly $373.5M in option awards, $26.6M in stock awards, and about $1.12M in base salary. The equity grants make most of the compensation contingent on future milestones.

The award is part of a broader pay plan unveiled in November that could be worth as much as $4.6B over a decade if performance conditions are met. In November, Tesla (TSLA) shareholders also approved a performance award of $1T for CEO Elon Musk. 

Rivian’s compensation committee said the structure aims to retain CEO Scaringe and align incentives with shareholders as the company enters its next phase, including scaling production and launching its R2 platform.

The options vest only if Rivian meets a series of ambitious targets tied to stock price, profitability, and cash generation. 

Equity tranches linked to share-price hurdles require the stock to rise from $40 to as high as $140 over sustained periods, which is a premium of up to 740% from stock's Monday close of $16.7. Additional portions depend on achieving adjusted operating income and cash flow from operations targets through 2032.

The company also scrapped a 2021 CEO performance award. The committee said the new structure better reflects current market conditions and Scaringe’s existing ownership stake while providing stronger incentives to execute on Rivian’s long-term strategy.

Scaringe, who founded Rivian in 2009 and led it to a 2021 public listing, remains central to the company’s push to transition from a startup to a scaled electric-vehicle manufacturer.Rivian shares have lost -15% value so far this year.

data.title: Smithfield Foods beats Q1 estimates
data.description:
• Smithfield Foods, Inc. press release (SFD): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.64 beats by $0.05.
  • Revenue of $3.8B (+0.5% Y/Y) beats by $100M.

  • The Company is reaffirming its financial outlook provided on March 24, 2026 as follows:

    • Total Company sales to be up low-single-digits compared to fiscal year 2025.
    • Packaged Meats segment adjusted operating profit of between $1,100 million to $1,200 million.
    • Fresh Pork segment adjusted operating profit of between $200 million to $260 million.
    • Hog Production segment adjusted operating profit of between $150 million to $200 million.
    • Total Company adjusted operating profit of between $1,325 million to $1,475 million.
